content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

September 23, 1994

Peter Bagatelos
Bagatelos and Fadem
601 California Street, Suite 1801
San Francisco, California 94108

RE: Your Request for Informal Assistance
     Our File No. I-94-162

Dear Mr. Bagatelos:

     This is in response to your letter requesting advice
concerning the campaign provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the "Act").¹ Since your request is one for informal assistance,
we can only provide the following informal response to your
questions pursuant to Regulation 18329(c)(4).²

     Please note that nothing in this letter should be construed
to evaluate any conduct which may have already taken place. In
addition, this letter is based on the facts presented to us. The
Commission does not act as the finder of fact in providing advice.
(In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

QUESTIONS

     1.    Does Section 84107 apply to independent expenditure
committees?

     2.    Does Section 84107 apply to a committee which supports
or opposes more than one ballot measure?

     3.    What constitutes "principal activity" for purposes of
Section 84107?

___________________________
¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections
18000 - 18954. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. I-94-162
Page 2


CONCLUSIONS

1.  Section 84107 applies to recipient committees,
independent expenditure committees, and major donor committees
under certain circumstances.

2.  Section 84107 may apply to committees which support or
oppose one or more ballot measures.

3.  The term "principal activity" is not defined in the Act
or any regulation of the Commission. Therefore, any reasonable
interpretation of this term is acceptable as described below.

FACTS

One of your clients is a nonprofit corporation which is
making independent expenditures to support two different measures
in the same city election. The client, which is filing Form 461
as an independent expenditure committee, is distributing mass
mailings expressing its views regarding the two measures and is
not making any other contributions or expenditures to support or
oppose candidates or measures in another jurisdiction.

ANALYSIS

In 1986, Section 84107's ballot measure identification
requirement was added to the Act by AB 688 (Johnston). The
Commission-sponsored bill added or amended various statutes to
ensure that the names of committees, such as sponsored and ballot
measure committees, reflected their true source of support.
(Assembly Third Reading Analysis, AB 688 (Johnston), dated June 6,
1985, and Senate Elections Committee Analysis dated July 17,
1985.)

Section 84107 provides the following:

Within 30 days of the designation of the
numerical order of propositions appearing on the
ballot, any committee which has as its principal
activity the support of or opposition to a ballot
measure, shall, if supporting the measure, include
the statement, "a committee for Proposition __,"
or, if opposing the measure, include the statement,
"a committee against Proposition __," in any
reference to the committee required by law.[4]

(Emphasis added.)

___________________________

3  A reference to a committee required by law includes
identification of the committee in a statement of organization,
campaign statements, and mass mailings. (Sections 84100-84305.)

Our File No. I-94-162
Page 3


Type of Committee

     The first question you pose is whether Section 84107 applies
to an independent expenditure committee. According to statute,
Section 84107 applies to "any" committee which has as
its principal activity the support of or opposition to a ballot
measure.

     We have not previously had occasion to apply Section 84107 to
an independent expenditure committee in an advice letter. Our
prior advice pertains only to primarily formed "recipient
committees." (Section 82013(a).) We have advised, for example:

     1.     The statute applies to recipient committees primarily
formed to support or oppose a ballot measure.5    (Powers Advice
Letter, No. A-86-221; Olson Advice Letter, A-89-304.)

     2.     Section 84107 applies to a committee formed primarily to
oppose the recall of a local elected official. (Hertz Advice
Letter, No. I-92-40.)

     However, the plain language of the statute does not limit its
scope to recipient committees. Rather, it applies to any
committee, if the committee's "principal activity" is the support
of or opposition to a ballot measure. Therefore, Section 84107
may apply to independent expenditure and major donor committees as
well.6




_________________________________
     4 Under the Act, a committee is broadly defined in Section 82013
as any person or combination of persons who directly or indirectly
receives contributions totaling $1,000 or more in a calendar year;
makes independent expenditures totaling $1,000 or more in a
calendar year; or makes contributions totaling $10,000 or more in
a calendar year to or at the behest of candidates or committees.

     5 While Section 84107 has generally been applied to committees
which support or oppose a single ballot measure, this stems
primarily from its frequent application to primarily formed
committees. Prior to 1991, only committees supporting a single
measure were considered "primarily formed." The definition of
"primarily formed committee" (Section 82047.5) was amended to
include two or more ballot measures being voted upon in the same
city, county, or state election. (Chapter 626, Statutes 1990 and
Chapter 191, Statutes 1991.)

     6 We recognize that due to our previous application of the
statute only to recipient committees, other committees may not be
aware of this requirement.

Our File No. I-94-162
Page 4


A Ballot Measure

     The second question you pose is whether Section 84107 applies
to an independent expenditure committee which supports two ballot
measures in a city election.

     The indefinite article "a" does not necessarily refer to a
singular or numerical term. (Lewis v. Spies (1973) 43 A.D.2d 714,
350 N.Y.S.2d 14.) It is often used in statutes to mean "each" or
"any" rather than "one." (Also see People v. Kirk (1989) 211
Cal.App.3d 58, 64; People v. Mackabee (1989) 214 Cal.App.3d 1250,
1261; Black's Law Dictionary, 5th ed., p. 1.)

     It does not appear that the term "a ballot measure" was
intended to exclude committees supporting and/or opposing more
than one measure. For example, a committee whose only previous
activity was expenditures of $100,000 in support of Measure A
could not avoid the application of Section 84107 simply by
spending $1,000 for Measure B. Its principal activity would still
be the support of Measure A. Thus, it would be required to
include the statement "A Committee for Measure A."

     The only issue is whether the committee's principal activity
involves a ballot measure.8 The fact that it supports other
measures or candidates is immaterial. Therefore, in the instant
case, in which a committee exists to support only two measures in
a city election, it appears that Section 84107 may apply, if the
support of either (or even both) is its "principal activity."

Principal Activity

     A final question you pose is what constitutes "principal
activity."9 This standard is not defined in the Act nor is it
defined in our regulations.

     The ordinary meaning of the term "principal" includes "chief;
leading; most important or considerable; primary; original."
(Black's Law Dictionary, 5th ed., p. 1073.) In addition, in
providing advice, we have used the term "primary," "primarily" and
"principal" interchangeably. For example, in the Olson Advice
Letter, supra, the term "primary" was used in interpreting the
term "principal" activity in Section 84107. Also, while Section
84102 refers to "primary" activity, we have referred to

     7    This is especially true where the committee supports one
measure, and opposes one or more competing measures.

     8    Please note that a more difficult question is posed when a
ballot measure committee supports or opposes two measures equally,
or supports multiples measures and opposes others.

     9    Commission staff may propose a regulation to define the term
"principal activity."

Our File No. I-94-162
Page 5


"principal" activity in discussing amendments to a statement of
organization. (Seibert Advice Letter, No. I-92-258; Rothman
Advice Letter, No. I-87-323; Berg Advice Letter, No. A-80-120.)

In your letter, you specifically ask about an independent
expenditure committee which intends to distribute mass mailings
expressing its views regarding two measures. It is not making any
other contributions or expenditures. Presumably, its "principal
activity" is to support at least one of the measures. Moreover,
you have not indicated that it will make a history of making
contributions relating to other candidates or measures.
Therefore, it appears that the "principal activity" of
the committee is to support a ballot measure and the requirements
of Section 84107 are applicable.

You also ask for general guidance in interpreting the term
"principal" obviously, if a committee's only activity from
inception is the support of a ballot measure, Section 84107 would
apply. Similarly, we also note that it would appear that Section
84107 would apply to an established "issues PAC" of a trade
association, union, or corporation. Such committees normally
would not have a principal activity of supporting a measure.

Without guidance from the Commission in the form of a
regulation or formal opinion, we regret that we cannot draw a
"bright line" in further interpreting the term. Therefore, we
believe that any additional general statements concerning the
scope of Section 84107 should be addressed in a future letter
where specific facts are presented.

If you have any questions regarding this letter, please
contact me at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

[signature]

By: Luisa Menchaca
     Counsel, Legal Division

CONCLUSION: 1.
Section 84107 applies to recipient committees,
independent expenditure committees, and maj or donor committees
under certain circumstances.
2.

The term “principal activity” is not defined in the Act
or any regulation of the Commission.
Therefore,
any reasonable
interpretation of this term is acceptable as described below.
